text.title: GraphQL Security: Depth Limits, Auth, Persisted Queries
text.summary: Protecting GraphQL APIs from DoS via depth/complexity limits and from data leaks via field-level authorization.

rule.statement: Add query depth limiting (max 10) and query complexity analysis (max 1000 points) before going to production.
rule.rationale: A malicious query like `user { friends { friends { friends { ... } } } }` can trigger exponential DB queries. Without limits, a single request can DoS the server.

rule.statement: Enforce pagination limits server-side: if a client requests more than the maximum page size, raise a validation error.
rule.rationale: Without server-side enforcement, a client requesting `first: 1000000` bypasses the pagination design and runs a full table scan.

rule.statement: Apply authorization at the field resolver level, not only at the gateway. Every resolver returning sensitive data must check the caller's identity.
rule.rationale: GraphQL composition allows arbitrary client queries. A gateway-level auth check on the query root does not protect individual fields returned as part of a larger query.

rule.statement: Use persisted queries (allowlist) for production clients. Reject unknown query documents by hash.
rule.rationale: Persisted queries eliminate injection risk (no dynamic query construction), enable CDN caching, and prevent introspection-based DoS from attackers enumerating the schema.
